data.title: Solventum Corporation 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Solventum Corporation in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Trump Taps Miran For Short-Term Fed Position
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Steve Miran, head of the Council of Economic Advisers, is expected to be another dovish voice at the FOMC. (0:15) OpenAI unveils GPT 5. (2:52) Union Pacific launches freight service connecting L.A. to Chicago to compete with trucking. (4:32)

The following is an abridged transcript:

President Donald Trump nominated Stephen Miran, chairman of the Council of Economic Advisers, to serve as a Federal Reserve governor until Jan. 31, 2026.

The president had said he'd likely nominate a temporary governor to fill the seat, rather than someone who would eventually be elevated to Fed chair. Miran, if approved by the Senate, will serve the remainder of Adriana Kugler's term.

"In the meantime, we will continue to search for a permanent replacement," Trump wrote on Truth Social late Thursday. "He will do an outstanding job."

Last week, Kugler said she'd step down from the Fed board as of Aug. 8 to resume her professorship at Georgetown University.

Miran has been a critic of the Fed in the past and also popularized the “Mar-a-Lago Accord” plan for a weaker dollar (DXY).

Pepperstone strategist Michael Brown says the nomination "reinforces what we were all thinking already, namely that what we now have is a much more political Fed, and a much less independent Fed, than we've seen for many, many decades. Naturally, that's going to continue to pose a structural headwind to the dollar, while in turn likely also boosting gold, as capital outflows from the States continue."

"Miran will have little-to-no credibility in the eyes of most market participants, including me, given that he will likely have pledged to vote for sizable rate cuts in order to have been nominated to fill the position," Brown said. "Hence, we know that he'll almost certainly be the most dovish 'dot' in the September SEP, and will probably be voting in favor of the largest cut he can plausibly make a case for at that meeting as well."

James Fishback, CEO at investment firm Azoria, says “I’ve known

Steve Miran for a couple of years now. He is 100% MAGA. He’s analytical (PhD in economics from Harvard) but he rolls up his sleeves. He talks to workers, small businesses, folks being affected by Jerome Powell’s awful policy of keeping interest rates near 20-year highs.”

But there is some incongruity with his past statements and his appointment by Trump, as well as the expectation that he will be voting for Trump’s preferred very-low-rates policy.

The Wall Street Journal noted that last year Miran criticized White House influence on the FOMC, saying: "Short-circuiting the revolving door between the Fed and the executive branch is critical to reducing the incentives for officials to act in the short-term political interests of the president."

And Bloomberg notes that in September he argued against cutting rates, saying that “massive easing” into GDP at 2.5%-3% with inflation also at 2.5% to 3% would mean inflation “cements” at those levels.

"The upside, if there is one, of all this, is that he's only going to be there for three meetings, limiting any 'damage' that can be done," Brown added. "That said, I think it's pretty clear that Miran's appointment sets the tone for how Trump is going to be looking at other Fed vacancies (Chair & permanent Kugler replacement), whereby loyalty to the president is the number 1 selection criterion."

OpenAI unveiled GPT‑5, which it says is its best AI system yet. The AI model has state-of-the-art performance across coding, math, writing, health, and visual perception, among other things, according to the company.

OpenAI said GPT‑5 is available to all users, with Plus subscribers getting more usage, and Pro subscribers getting access to GPT‑5 pro, a version with extended reasoning for even more comprehensive and accurate answers.

Microsoft (MSFT) CEO Satya Nadella said: "Today, GPT-5 launches across our platforms, including Microsoft 365 Copilot, Copilot, GitHub Copilot, and Azure AI Foundry. It's the most capable model yet from our partners at OpenAI, bringing powerful new advances in reasoning, coding, and chat, all trained on Azure."

GPT‑5 is a unified system with a smart, efficient model that answers most questions, a deeper reasoning model (GPT‑5 thinking) for harder problems, and a real‑time router that quickly decides which to use based on conversation type, complexity, tool needs, and explicit intent, the company added.

Elon Musk responded to Nadella on X, saying: “OpenAI is going to eat Microsoft alive.”

Meanwhile, Bloomberg reports that Tesla (TSLA) is dismantling its Dojo supercomputer team.

Peter Bannon, who led the Dojo project, is leaving the company, while remaining team members will be reassigned to other data centers and compute initiatives within Tesla. This follows the recent departure of around 20 Tesla employees who left to form a new AI startup, DensityAI.

Musk said on X, “It doesn’t make sense for Tesla to divide its resources and scale two quite different AI chip designs.” He added that all efforts will now be focused on Tesla’s upcoming AI5, AI6, and future chip architectures.

And Union Pacific (UNP) plans to launch a new, truck-competitive domestic intermodal service connecting Southern California's Inland Empire to Chicago.

The service is expected to significantly boost the rail company's intermodal capacity. The new offering is part of Union Pacific's Z train network, which is said to provide the fastest delivery of time-sensitive freight.

The company said the service will connect from the Los Angeles Basin's most active warehouse district through Union Pacific’s Inland Empire Intermodal Terminal directly to Chicago's Global 2 Intermodal Terminal, beginning on September 3.

Notably, rail customers will experience up to 20% faster intermodal service compared to current industry offerings between the key locations, with three days' transit. The service will start at five days a week with the ability to increase with growth.

Now Here’s What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

The Trade Desk (TTD) is shedding nearly a third of its value after a lackluster Q2 report and the exit of its CFO.

Intel’s (INTC) CEO reaffirms his commitment to the company amid Trump’s calls for his to resign.

And Meta (META) is reportedly tapping Blue Owl (OWL) and PIMCO for $29 billion in financing for a data center project.

Taking a look at the markets, stock index futures (SPX) (INDU) (US100:IND) are slightly higher, bolstered by tech gains. But lingering tariff concerns are limiting gains. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) is easing back to around 4.25%.

Among active stocks premarket, Take-Two Interactive (TTWO) is higher after beating key metrics in its latest earnings report. Block (XYZ) is also up post-earnings as it raised guidance. Pinterest (PINS) is slumping after it missed on the bottom line.

The economic calendar is clear today.

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which is no guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value are historical and may fluctuate so that redemption value may be worth more or less than the original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than what is quoted. Call 1-866-AVE-MARIA for the most current month-end performance.

The Fund's exposure to dividend-paying common stocks contributed to its underperformance versus the benchmark during the first half of the year. Notable performers include Coca-Cola Europacific Partners PLC (CCEP) (non-alcoholic beverages), Fastenal Company (FAST) (industrial wholesale & rental) and Texas Instruments, Inc. (TXN) (semiconductor devices), which increased by 22%, 18%, and 13%, respectively. Detractors to performance included the common stocks of Western Union Company (WU) (financial transaction processor), Diamondback Energy, Inc. (FANG) (exploration & production) and Watsco, Inc. (WSO) (industrial wholesale & rental), which decreased by -17%, -15%, and -6%, respectively.

Interest rates on the intermediate portion of the yield curve declined during the first half of the year, while short-term and long-term rates remained relatively stable. Markets continue to grapple with the effects of persistent inflation, newly imposed tariffs, and the long-term implications of sustained deficit spending out of Washington, D.C. The Federal Reserve has maintained a holding pattern, adopting a wait-and-see approach as it evaluates incoming economic data before determining its next policy move.

Corporate bond spreads widened in April but ultimately ended the first half of the year close to where they began, near historically low levels. This dynamic calls for caution when considering additional credit exposure, particularly in lower quality issuers where investors are not being adequately compensated for the elevated credit risk.

The Bond Fund will continue to be managed in a conservative manner by keeping bond maturities in the short-to-intermediate range and the credit quality high. Additionally, high-quality, dividend-paying common stocks continue to offer an attractive combination of income and price appreciation potential.

Important Information For Investors

As of 6-30-25, the holding percentages of the stocks mentioned in this commentary are as follows: Coca-Cola Europacific Partners PLC (1.9%), Fastenal Company (1.2%), Texas Instruments, Inc. (1.3%), Diamondback Energy, Inc., (0.8%) and Watsco, Inc. (1.5%). Fund holdings are subject to change and should not be considered purchase recommendations. There is no assurance that the securities mentioned remain in the Fund’s portfolio or that securities sold have not been repurchased. Fund holdings are subject to change and should not be considered purchase recommendations. The Fund’s top ten holdings as of 6-30-25: Coca-Cola Europacific Partners (1.9%), Watsco, Inc. (1.5%), The Western Union Company (1.4%), Illinois Tool Works, Inc. 2.65% due 11/15/26 (1.4%), U.S. Treasury Note 4.625% due 09/30/30 (1.4%), U.S. Treasury Note 4.50% due 11/15/33 (1.4%), U.S. Treasury Note 4.375% due 01/31/32 (1.4%), U.S. Treasury Note 4.250% due 06/30/29 (1.4%), U.S. Treasury Note 4.125% due 07/31/28 (1.3%), U.S. Treasury Note 4.125% due 07/31/31 (1.3%). data.title: Wasatch Small Cap Value Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

The U.S. equity market finished with positive total returns in the second quarter, albeit with a high level of volatility. In early April, President Donald Trump unveiled a tariff plan with a far more protectionist bent than market participants had been expecting. Stocks plunged in response as investors began to factor in an environment of slower economic growth and higher inflation. This news was particularly damaging for small caps, since companies in the category tend to have above-average economic sensitivity. The picture changed significantly within a week after President Trump announced a 90-day pause on new tariffs. Investors appeared to conclude that the initial announcement was largely a negotiating ploy. The pause restored confidence and helped the market stage a quick recovery. As the quarter progressed and data related to economic growth, inflation and corporate earnings exhibited stability, stocks marched steadily higher. While major large-cap indexes achieved new highs, small cap value stocks lagged somewhat due to underperformance early in the period.

One notable element of the rally was the leadership displayed by lower-quality stocks amid investors’ effort to play catch-up with the rising market. Although we didn’t participate in this move due to our emphasis on quality, we nonetheless outperformed the Russell 2000® Value Index, which gained 4.97%. The Wasatch Small Cap Value Fund— Investor Class gained 5.72% in the second quarter. We believe the Fund’s strength relative to the benchmark in a potentially challenging period helps to illustrate the merits of our rigorous stock-selection process and our bottom-up focus on company fundamentals. Despite investors’ recent rotation into lower quality stocks, we maintain a high-quality bias in the Fund. While it’s impossible to say whether an emphasis on quality will be rewarded in any specific quarter or period, we see it as the best approach to small cap value investing over the long term.

Details of the quarter

The Fund typically has a more growth-oriented bent than the broader small cap value category. This aspect of our approach played a key role in the Fund’s second quarter results, as many of our top contributors to performance were in higher-growth sectors such as information technology, consumer discretionary and health care.

In technology, two of the primary contributors were AI-related stocks that had lagged in the first three months of the year but have since come back to life amid renewed enthusiasm about the artificial intelligence theme. Fabrinet (FN) and Nova Ltd. (NVMI) stand to benefit from the buildout of AI infrastructure. We maintained these positions through the first-quarter downturn since the companies’ fundamentals remained strong. Arlo Technologies, Inc. (ARLO), which provides do-it-yourself home security monitoring, also performed well after announcing that its subscription base had grown by more than 20%.

Universal Technical Institute, Inc. (UTI), a for-profit education provider, played a key role in the Fund’s relative strength in the consumer-discretionary sector. The company benefited from a weaker job market, accelerating fundamentals, and the potential for reduced regulation. Discount retailer Five Below, Inc. (FIVE) was another top contributor in the sector, as was Frontdoor, Inc. (FTDR), a provider of residential service plans covering homeowners for issues with appliances, HVAC systems, and other areas.

Two holdings drove the Fund’s strong performance in health care. Health Equity, Inc. (HQY) was the top contributor in the sector. The company benefited from solid financial results and signs that it’s successfully addressing a previous fraud issue. Ensign Group, Inc. (ENSG) contributed as the company’s steady characteristics have come into favor with investors.

On the other end of the spectrum, many of the Fund’s largest detractors for the quarter were companies with above-average sensitivity to macroeconomic trends. Homebuilders LGI Homes, Inc. (LGHI) and Champion Homes, Inc. (SKY) saw their stocks weaken on concerns that persistently high interest rates would crimp housing demand. Similarly, UFP Industries, Inc. (UFPI)—a wood processing company whose business is tied to homebuilding—and National Storage Affiliates Trust (NSA), a storage property real estate investment trust, whose growth tends to fluctuate in line with housing turnover, were also notable laggards. Trucking company Saia, Inc. (SAIA), energy producer Magnolia Oil & Gas Corp. (MGY) and Kadant, Inc. (KAI), a supplier of machinery used in industrial processes such as paper production, were additional holdings affected by economic growth concerns. It’s important to keep in mind that these positions don’t reflect an attempt on our part to tilt the Fund’s portfolio in a cyclical direction, but rather they’re the result of our ongoing effort to achieve balance by keeping a portion of the Fund invested in stocks that could benefit if the economy accelerates.

Outlook and positioning

Striving to use stock-price volatility to add quality companies to the Fund at attractive valuations has been an important aspect of managing the Small Cap Value Fund over time. We continued to pursue this approach by using recent market fluctuations to add a position in CSW Industrials, Inc. (CSW), a provider of HVAC and plumbing components. CSW’s stock had weakened on concerns about slowing new construction, obscuring the fact that its business is primarily tied to the repair and replacement cycle. We also added Globant SA (GLOB), whose stock we think has been excessively punished by worries about competition from AI. We invested in Shift4 Payments, Inc. (FOUR), as we believe management changes have caused investors to overlook the company’s favorable fundamental profile. Another addition of note was Freshpet, Inc. (FRPT), a company we’ve followed for a long time whose stock has recently come under pressure due to a modest slowdown in top-line growth. To maintain portfolio balance and because these holdings tend to have a cyclical tilt, we funded the purchase of their stocks by selling other economically sensitive holdings that we believed had become more fully valued.(Current and future holdings are subject to risk and change.)

Our emphasis on balance is in part a reflection of our view that the economy and markets are subject to a wide range of possible outcomes as we move into the second half of the year. On the positive side, a “soft landing” with continued low inflation, moderate growth, and accommodative U.S. Federal Reserve policy would represent an ideal result. On the other end of the spectrum, uncertainty surrounding trade and geopolitics could feed through to the economy and lead to stagflation. As bottom-up investors, we prefer to focus on company fundamentals and balanced portfolio construction rather than attempting to predict the path of the economy. This approach has held us in good stead over time, and we believe it remains particularly appropriate in the current environment.

Thank you for the opportunity to manage your assets.

Sincerely, Austin Bone

The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the data quoted. For the most recent month-end performance data, visit wasatchglobal.com. data.title: Artisan Partners: Difficult To Look Past Near-Term Earnings Pressure
data.description:
Artisan Partners Asset Management (APAM) is a traditional investment manager with ~$167bn of assets under management (or AUM) across a range of active strategies. The differentiator here is its “talent-driven business model”, which emphasizes acquiring ‘star’ portfolio management teams and giving them autonomy within the Artisan platform. In contrast, most other US-listed asset managers take on a more firm-oriented approach and manage around their respective benchmarks.

Key here is that Artisan’s unique approach comes with more value-add, as evidenced by its above-par performance results. This, in turn, adds a layer of insulation to the outflow pressures faced across the industry.

That said, changes are afoot at Artisan now that it has a newly promoted CEO, Jason Gottlieb, in place. Early signals point to this shift bringing new growth ambitions – especially on the inorganic front. With the dividend yield also up to a seemingly attractive ~7%, it’s as good a time as any, in my view, for a closer look at the APAM investment case.

New CEO, New Growth Promise

Artisan’s recently held Q2 2025 call was a first for new CEO Gottlieb. Recall that Artisan had, earlier this year, announced its senior management transition plan - Jason Gottlieb, formerly President & COO of Investments, would take over from outgoing CEO Eric Colson, now Executive Chair, after the company’s annual meeting in June. In all, a well-telegraphed shift and, on paper, one that comes with strategic continuity (note new CEO Gottlieb has been at Artisan since 2016 and overseen its “thoughtful growth”).

What the new CEO Gottlieb might bring to the table, though, is a bigger push on the growth drivers we’ve seen in recent years. For context, since 2013, when Artisan IPO’d and more so since 2016, when Gottlieb joined, Artisan has expanded beyond ‘vanilla’ equity strategies into credit (high yield, long/short, emerging markets), global macro, and private markets. This has translated into more than double the revenue generation across eleven investment teams and twenty-six strategies.

From here, Artisan will adopt the same “talent driven” approach when it comes to M&A or “lift-outs” (i.e., acquiring portfolio management teams instead of entire firms). But instead of public equity, expect the focus to be on alternative assets - per Gottlieb on the Q2 2025 call, “M&A is a potent opportunity for us in the future, specifically in the alternatives asset classes”.

Management also called out a few specific areas within alternatives. Firstly, real estate (“it's highly fragmented, there's multiple ways to win, there's a lot of talent out there”) and secondly, private equity secondaries (“risk can be taken, risk can be rewarded”). Also in the mix is private credit, specifically asset-based lending (“vast TAM, big opportunity set and high differentiation”).

Funding-wise, Artisan’s focus on more niche parts of private assets and its preference for buying managers, rather than firms, means it should have more than enough financial capacity to execute. As of Q2 2025, the firm had $245m cash on a lowly geared balance sheet (0.5x leverage ratio).

What is harder to underwrite, though, is whether Artisan can replicate the playbook that worked so well before (identifying, buying out, and building a team around a ‘star’ manager) in private markets. Note that the company is untested here, as evidenced by its much smaller private platform (~2% of AUM) vs. public assets, and competition from bigger platforms is ramping up. It is also coming off the shutdown of its $113m China post-venture fund that, per Reuters, “posted a net loss of 10.4% since its inception in March 2021”.

For now, investors will have to ‘wait and see’. The Artisan playbook typically takes years to scale, so meaningful results won’t come through for a while. In the meantime, I’d keep a close eye on any performance numbers that come through, progress on scaling these new platforms while limiting attrition, as well as any margin tradeoffs incurred in the process.

No Escaping The Secular Outflows

For all the future growth promise, some perspective is important. Artisan’s core business remains actively managed equities - an asset class especially vulnerable to attrition. Its latest Q2 report, for instance, showed total net outflows of $1.9bn. While slower than the $2.8bn of outflows suffered in Q1, this still brings Artisan’s year-to-date outflow run-rate to over double the whole of last year’s.

Per management, the key culprit here was the same “separate account rebalancing” that led to a $1.2bn drag on Q1 flows. Beyond that, a lot of attrition also came from Artisan’s equities business, with credit and emerging market inflows only partially offsetting these losses. There was also attrition in the much smaller alternatives business, mostly thanks to the >$100m China post-venture strategy wind down. Even excluding this one-off, though, flows into alternatives, while positive, have been relatively small.

To be fair, management did also disclose the onboarding of two emerging market institutional mandates for the EMsights and Sustainable Emerging Markets platforms. No word, however, on the size of these new mandates nor the timeline of their funding. Thus, it’s probably best not to underwrite anything too significant at this point; certainly nothing material enough to offset the headwinds across its legacy equity strategies.

Holding Steady On Fees For Now

The other key revenue variable for asset managers is the fees they charge on their assets. This is really where Artisan shines relative to its peers. As of Q2 2025, weighted average management fee yield came in ahead of peers at 68bps; this represented only ~1bps of compression vs. last year, so a very resilient outcome as well.

To be clear, some of this is down to mix - Artisan over-indexes toward global and international equity strategies, which command higher (albeit compressing) fee rates. But the firm also deserves credit for its value add, with virtually all of its long-running strategies outperforming their benchmarks.

From here, defending its overall fee rate will depend on how quickly Artisan can expand into alternative products. Note that these come with even higher fee rates than equities – as of Q2 2025, Artisan’s alternative strategies produced a 79bps management fee yield (vs. 69bps for equities). Alternatives are also experiencing inflows, so unlike equities, the growth runway is a lot more attractive and comes with much less fee pressure.

Margins Under Pressure

Artisan has also been defending margins amid the outflows, though efforts here have not been enough to offset another quarter of compression. To recap, the manager’s Q2 2025 operating margin stood at 31.7% - below Street estimates and ~40bps down on last quarter.

A closer look shows that it was higher compensation expenses that drove this big miss. On the non-compensation side, management actually made cuts to general & administrative and occupancy spending.

In the near term, Artisan can probably lean on more non-comp cost cuts to defend margins; though having already tapped into this lever in prior quarters, I suspect this runway is a lot narrower. Unless the massive year-to-date outflows in equities turn around (unlikely anytime soon), Artisan will have a challenging time offsetting this year’s “mid-single digits” % operating expense growth guide, in my view.

Summing Up

Net-net, for all of Artisan’s differentiating qualities, there’s no escaping the secular pressures being faced across the industry. While new management has its hopes pinned on long-term alternatives-led growth, it’s hard to look past the near-term earnings pressure, which, in turn, isn’t being sufficiently priced into the premium ~13x P/E multiple, in my view. Even with an ~7% dividend yield on offer, I’m not keen on chasing the stock.

data.title: Fortrea Holdings: What Is There To Like About This Situation
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Curreen Capital Partners Q2 2025 Letter.

Fortrea Holdings (NASDAQ:FTRE) is a contract research organization (CRO), which means they run clinical trials to test treatments that pharmaceutical companies are trying to get approved for use in patients. I have been following Fortrea since it spun out of Labcorp (LH) in June 2023. Back then, Fortrea seemed like an exciting, fast-growing business earning good returns on capital, with a star CEO who had a reputation for running (and selling) a similar business. Things have fallen apart since then…

Compared to the exciting period after COVID, fervor for new drug research has declined. Less money has flowed to biotech companies, who hire CROs to help run the clinical trials they need in order to market their therapies. In addition to this industry-wide headwind, Fortrea has gone through the expensive process of setting up its own IT systems and operations separate from Labcorp. Fortrea also has a decent amount of debt, which is common in spinoffs. All in, this once exciting business ran into an industry slowdown, increased spending on stand-up costs, and still had all that debt to deal with. Earnings turned to losses, the stock price dropped and kept dropping, and as of mid-May the star CEO was out.

The positives are that Fortrea has been winning new clinical trials and growing its backlog. The company has nearly completed the stand-up of its own systems and separation from Labcorp’s – which means they can stop paying for two systems, reducing expenses. The financial picture is ugly, but I think that it is improving and that the company will avoid a painful financial restructuring. Also, the stock is priced for disaster.

On the other side of the current unpleasantness is a good business in a good industry. Running clinical trials is not capital intensive – the drug company customer supplies the medicine, and the contract research organization finds investigators (doctors) who generally see the trial participants in the doctors’ own offices. While this capital-light model allows contract research organizations to earn good returns on capital, there are barriers to entry for would-be competitors. CROs have to know the science and regulations involved in getting drugs approved in different jurisdictions worldwide, they need to know and have a good reputation with doctors who can work on the clinical trials and bring in patients. And in addition to this know-how and know-who, drug companies need to feel confident that they are hiring a CRO who will give them a solid shot at getting their drug approved for use. Getting a new drug approved is a long and expensive process – and the drug company’s main goal is to maximize the time that their therapy is on-market and patent-protected. Anything that delays FDA approval—be it signing up investigators, recruiting and retaining patients, analyzing and presenting data, and generally jumping through regulatory hoops—eats into that patent- protected period and costs Fortrea’s customers a lot of money. Going with a lower priced new entrant, vs going with Fortrea or one of the other large players with a track record, is risky for the pharmaceutical company customer (and introduces career risk for the individual making the choice) because the new entrant may delay—by months, years, or even permanently—approval by the FDA and other regulators.

New drug research should continue as long as patents are legally protected and valuable, and the aging global population increases demand for new treatments. I do not see the current political climate changing either of these long-term drivers. Increasing demand is met by a limited supply of CROs with a track record, which sets the industry up for continued growth at high returns on capital. Historically, CROs have used their free cash flow to grow and to buy back stock. While turnarounds often take more time than I would like, Fortrea should make it back to being a growing business earning high returns on capital, and that might even earn it an exciting earnings multiple. I think they are on track.

We paid $5.85 per share for our position in Fortrea, which was about 4.5% of the fund. I thought that price was extremely cheap, though the stock has since dropped further.

Disclaimer

The information and opinions expressed herein are as of the date appearing in this material only, are not complete, are subject to change without prior notice, and do not contain material information regarding the Fund, including specific information relating to an investment in the Fund and related important risk disclosures. This document is not intended to be, nor should it be construed or used as an offer to sell, or a solicitation of any offer to buy any interests in the Fund. If any offer is made, it shall be pursuant to a definitive Private Placement Memorandum prepared by or on behalf of the Fund which contains detailed information concerning the investment terms and the risks, fees and expenses associated with an investment in the Fund.

An investment in the Fund is speculative and may involve substantial investment and other risks. Such risks may include, without limitation, risk of adverse or unanticipated market developments, risk of counterparty or issuer default, and risk of illiquidity. The performance results of the Fund can be volatile. data.title: Aristotle Focus Growth Q2 Highlights: Top Performers, Surprises And Strategic Shifts
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Aristotle Focus Growth Q2 2025 Commentary.

Contributors and Detractors for 2Q 2025

Relative Contributors Relative Detractors
Adaptive Biotechnologies (ADPT) UnitedHealth Group (UNH)
Netflix (NFLX)

Visa (V)
Trane Technologies (TT) Bio-Techne (TECH)
Broadcom (AVGO) S&P Global (SPGI)
CrowdStrike Holdings (CRWD) Costco Wholesale (COST)

Detractors

UnitedHealth Group

UnitedHealth Group detracted from relative performance in the second quarter following a surprisingly soft first quarter earnings report and a sizable guidance reduction. The issues were two-fold, including higher utilization inside its group Medicare Advantage offering, which drove a higher medical care ratio. The second issue was a negative new member mix, as several competitors exited the market, and many of the patients who switched to UnitedHealth’s offerings had not been actively engaged in their care, resulting in higher acuity levels throughout the quarter. The company believes both issues are fixable in its next rate cycle.

Visa

Visa detracted from relative performance in the second quarter due to growing concerns that stablecoins could pose a long-term threat to the company’s payments business. While consumer adoption of stablecoins remains limited at present, pending U.S. legislation viewed as favorable to the cryptocurrency ecosystem, along with interest from large merchants exploring the issuance of proprietary stablecoins, has highlighted potential disruption risks to parts of Visa’s core network.

Contributors

Adaptive Biotechnologies

Adaptive Biotechnologies contributed to relative performance in the second quarter following a better-than-expected first quarter earnings report, which showed solid across-the-board momentum in both clinical testing and biopharma testing volumes. Adaptive continues to make progress on growing average selling prices (ASPS) and reducing cash burn. Adaptive is also rolling out its test on electronic medical record platforms, which will simplify the workflow and increase test ordering, processing and reporting efficiency.

Netflix

Netflix contributed to relative performance in the second quarter, primarily due to first quarter earnings results that supported investor confidence in continued growth. In particular, additional price increases in select markets, healthy subscriber momentum and progress in the nascent ad-supported subscription tier also helped drive performance. The company reiterated its full-year targets, with no signs of weakness in domestic or international markets.

Recent Portfolio Activity

The table below shows all buys and sells completed during the quarter, followed by a brief rationale.

Buys Sells
HubSpot (HUBS) Expedia Group (EXPE)

Buys

HubSpot

HubSpot provides a cloud-based customer relationship management (CRM) platform that integrates marketing, sales, service, content management and operations hubs into a unified system. Its platform features applications and tools for tasks such as website creation, business blogging, search engine optimization, web analytics, lead generation and more, while also offering a native payment solution. Designed to create adaptable and cohesive customer experiences, HubSpot’s Smart CRM and engagement hubs facilitate unified customer profiles and seamless interaction across the customer lifecycle. The hubs, available in free and paid tiers, can function independently, integrate with HubSpot’s Smart platform or third-party CRMs, and scale alongside growing businesses.

We invested in HubSpot for its strategic positioning in a large, underserved SMB market, its effective freemium-to-enterprise conversion funnel and its rapidly growing AI suite “Breeze,” which enhances automation and personalization across customer interactions. With strong adoption across product tiers, momentum in enterprise expansion, and scalable financials driving margin expansion and free cash flow growth, we believe the company is well-positioned to deliver durable, long-term value. We believe the current valuation is fair based on the combination of durable revenue growth, expanding margins and the early signs of traction in AI monetization.

Sells

Expedia Group

We sold Expedia Group due to a higher risk of a recession following the significant increase in global tariffs, which can be expected to reduce overall levels of economic activity. In addition, the negative impact on the wealth effect due to potentially declining equity markets will likely impact demand for retail travel. Travel is among the most discretionary spend categories within the consumer discretionary sector.

Disclosures

The opinions expressed herein are those of Aristotle Atlantic Partners, LLC (Aristotle Atlantic) and are subject to change without notice. Past performance is not a guarantee or indicator of future results. This material is not financial advice or an offer to purchase or sell any product. You should not assume that any of the securities transactions, sectors or holdings discussed in this report were or will be profitable, or that recommendations Aristotle Atlantic makes in the future will be profitable or equal the performance of the listed in this report. The portfolio characteristics shown relate to the Aristotle Atlantic Focus Growth strategy. Not every client’s account will have these characteristics. Aristotle Atlantic reserves the right to modify its current investment strategies and techniques based on changing market dynamics or client needs. There is no assurance that any securities discussed herein will remain in an account’s portfolio at the time you receive this report or that securities sold have not been repurchased. The securities discussed may not represent an account’s entire portfolio and, in the aggregate, may represent only a small percentage of an account’s portfolio holdings. The performance attribution presented is of a representative account from Aristotle Atlantic’s Focus Growth Composite. The representative account is a discretionary client account which was chosen to most closely reflect the investment style of the strategy. The criteria used for representative account selection is based on the account’s period of time under management and its similarity of holdings in relation to the strategy. Recommendations made in the last 12 months are available upon request. Returns are presented gross and net of investment advisory fees and include the reinvestment of all income. Gross returns will be reduced by fees and other expenses that may be incurred in the management of the account. Net returns are presented net of actual investment advisory fees and after the deduction of all trading expenses.

International investments involve special risks that may in particular cause a loss in principal, including currency fluctuation, lower liquidity, different accounting methods and economic and political systems, and higher transaction costs. These risks typically are greater in emerging markets. While Large-capitalization companies may have more stable prices than smaller, less established companies, they are still subject to equity securities risk. In addition, large-capitalization equity security prices may not rise as much as prices of equity securities of small-capitalization companies. Securities of small- and medium-sized companies tend to have a shorter history of operations, be more volatile and less liquid. Value stocks can perform differently from the market as a whole and other types of stocks. Index Disclosures

The Russell 1000® Growth Index measures the performance of the large cap growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. This index has been selected as the benchmark and is used for comparison purposes only. The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower expected growth values. The S&P 500® Index is the Standard & Poor’s Composite Index of 500 stocks and is a widely recognized, unmanaged index of common stock prices. The Russell 2000® Index measures the performance of the small cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. The Russell 2000 Index is a subset of the Russell 3000® Index representing approximately 10% of the total market capitalization of that index. It includes approximately 2,000 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market cap and current index membership. The Dow Jones Industrial Average® is a price-weighted measure of 30 U.S. blue-chip companies. The Index covers all industries except transportation and utilities. The NASDAQ Composite Index measures all NASDAQ domestic and international based common type stocks listed on The NASDAQ Stock Market. The NASDAQ Composite includes over 3,000 companies, more than most other stock market indices. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is an unmanaged index of domestic investment grade bonds, including corporate, government and mortgage-backed securities. The WTI Crude Oil Index is a major trading classification of sweet light crude oil that serves as a major benchmark price for oil consumed in the United States. The 3-Month U.S. Treasury Bill is a short-term debt obligation backed by the U.S. Treasury Department with a maturity of three months. The U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) is a measure of the value of the U.S. dollar relative to the value of a basket of currencies of the majority of the United States’ most significant trading partners. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) is a measure of the average change over time in the prices paid by urban consumers for a market basket of consumer goods and services. While stock selection is not governed by quantitative rules, a stock typically is added only if the company has an excellent reputation, demonstrates sustained growth and is of interest to a large number of investors. The volatility (beta) of the Composite may be greater or less than its respective benchmarks. It is not possible to invest directly in these indices.

The Fund’s best-performing stocks during the six-month period included Wheaton Precious Metals Corporation (WPM) (+60%), Franco-Nevada Corporation (FNV) (+40%) and VeriSign, Inc. (VRSN) (+40%). The main detractors from performance included Simply Good Foods Company, Inc. (SMPL) (-19%), YETI Holdings, Inc. (YETI) (-18%) and Chemed Corporation (CHE) (-13%).

The Fund owns a diversified portfolio of 31 companies across a broad array of industries with an emphasis on industrials, energy, royalties, and real estate. Texas Pacific Land Corporation (TPL) remains the largest holding in the Fund at 13.9% of assets. The Fund recently established two new positions: Builders FirstSource, Inc. (BLDR) (Building Products & Equipment) and Roper Technologies, Inc. (ROP) (Software). The Fund continues to be managed with a long-term focus, using a value-oriented investment approach based upon fundamental security analysis.
